claims: Rust compiles generics by monomorphization, replacing generic code with concrete type-specific versions for actually used types.
The Rust Book claims generic types do not make runtime execution slower than concrete types in the illustrated sense.
Generic operations require compile-time trait bounds, so the compiler knows available operations for T.
caveats: The source does not discuss code size, compile-time cost, instruction-cache effects, or optimizer pass details.

claims: Trait objects use dyn Trait behind a pointer to a value plus a method table looked up at runtime.
Generics use static dispatch by monomorphization when concrete types are known; trait objects use dynamic dispatch when concrete types are not known to the compiler.
Dynamic dispatch has runtime lookup cost and prevents method-body inlining, which can prevent some optimizations.
Trait objects enable heterogeneous collections checked against trait implementation at compile time.
caveats: Book describes conceptual table rather than stable ABI/vtable layout; dynamic dispatch is a flexibility/performance tradeoff, not universally bad.

claims: Only dyn-compatible traits may be base traits of trait objects.
Dyn-compatible traits cannot require Self: Sized, cannot have associated constants, and dispatchable methods cannot have type parameters or opaque return types such as async fn or impl Trait.
Dispatchable receivers are limited to specified pointer receiver forms such as &Self, &mut Self, Box<Self>, Rc<Self>, Arc<Self>, and Pin over them.
Methods with where Self: Sized may exist but are explicitly non-dispatchable through the trait object.
caveats: These are language restrictions for dynamic dispatch compatibility, not direct optimizer guarantees.

claims: Trait objects are dynamically sized types used behind pointers; each pointer to a trait object includes a data pointer and a vtable pointer.
The vtable contains method pointers for the concrete type implementation of the base trait and supertraits.
Calling a method on a trait object performs virtual dispatch at runtime by loading a function pointer from the vtable and invoking it.
Trait object type identity includes base trait, auto-trait set, and lifetime bound.
caveats: The Reference does not specify exact ABI layout, entry ordering, address stability, or devirtualization rules.

claims: More codegen units allow LLVM parallelism and may speed compile times, but may produce slower code; codegen-units=1 may improve generated code performance while slowing compilation.
Incremental compilation can inhibit certain optimizations and is not recommended for release builds.
LTO enables LLVM whole-program analysis for better optimized code at longer link time; linker-plugin LTO can defer optimization to the linker.
caveats: The page does not enumerate which optimizations are lost or tie this specifically to generics/monomorphization.

claims: CIL for generic types contains metadata identifying type parameters.
For value type generic arguments, the runtime creates a specialized generic type for each unique value type, avoiding conversions because the specialized class natively contains the value type.
For reference type generic arguments, the runtime creates/reuses one specialized version with object references because all references are the same size.
Reflection can query generic type information and actual type parameters at runtime.
caveats: This is runtime representation evidence; it does not by itself state JIT inlining/devirtualization behavior or code-size tradeoffs beyond sharing reference-type instantiations.

claims: CLR has access to generic type information at runtime, and reflection can obtain generic type and method information.
Reflection APIs expose whether a type/method is generic, its generic arguments, generic type definition, constraints, and constructed methods/types.
caveats: Reflection availability may constrain ahead-of-time/link-time assumptions unless closed-world restrictions are imposed; the source does not discuss optimizer effects directly.

claims: sealed on a class prevents other classes from inheriting from it.
sealed on an overriding method/property prevents further overrides while still allowing derivation from the class.
Structs are implicitly sealed.
caveats: Microsoft page does not explicitly claim devirtualization/inlining benefits, so only semantic finality should be recorded.

claims: GCC -fdevirtualize can convert calls to virtual functions into direct calls and is enabled at O2/O3/Os.
GCC can speculatively devirtualize likely target sets into guarded direct calls plus fallback indirect calls.
Inlining, indirect inlining, IPA constant propagation, IPA SRA, IPA ICF, and other interprocedural analyses are enabled at optimization levels and can interact with dispatch optimization.
LTO stores GIMPLE in object files and merges objects at link time as if part of one translation unit, enabling cross-file IPA and inlining.
Semantic interposition can restrict IPA/inlining/propagation for exported ELF symbols; disabling it lets GCC assume interposed symbols preserve semantics.
caveats: Options are compiler- and target-dependent; speculative calls can be reverted; some IPA options cost memory/compile time or code size.

claims: Clang builds on the LLVM optimizer and code generator, and the optimizer assumes code has no undefined behavior.
Optimization remarks can report inlining and missed/performed optimization decisions through Rpass flags.
LTO/ThinLTO are mentioned in optimization-record and CFI contexts; excerpt did not provide broad devirtualization claims.
caveats: The fetched excerpt did not support devirtualization/whole-program-vtables claims; avoid inventing them from memory.

claims: Julia performance depends strongly on type inference and type-stable functions whose return types are predictable.
Changing variable types, abstract fields, abstract containers, and Function-typed fields hide representation/call targets and can require dynamic dispatch or indirect storage.
Function barriers move hot loops behind concrete argument types so Julia can specialize them.
Julia may avoid specializing on Type, Function, and Vararg arguments in some forwarding-only cases; type parameters can force specialization.
Values-as-parameters can expose compile-time values such as dimensions, but overuse can create many specializations and compile-time/cache pressure.
Closures can box captured variables and cause runtime dispatch when stable type/non-reassignment cannot be proven.
caveats: Performance tips are practical guidance; actual specialization depends on compiler heuristics and workload.

claims: Julia dispatch selects the most specific applicable method based on number and types of all positional arguments; keyword arguments do not participate.
A broad method may be compiled separately for concrete argument tuples, allowing downstream method calls to be resolved ahead of execution.
Julia may avoid specialization where possible cases are effectively unlimited.
Ambiguous overlapping methods raise MethodError rather than relying on definition order.
World age can make newly added methods unavailable to currently running code.
caveats: methods(f) lists user-visible methods, not compiler-generated specializations; internal method-table layout is not described.
